sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 10 actions in the previous sequence.

Current state: step 36, level 1.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The blocks are at rows 40-44, cols 34-38. The LEFT action didn't move the blocks - they're constrained to gray areas. The right gray area (cols 34-51) is connected to the left gray area (cols 14-28) via the horizontal bar at rows 25-29. To reach the white cross at rows 31-33, cols 21-22, I need to go UP to the horizontal bar, LEFT, then DOWN into the left gray area.
- Recent findings: After RIGHT then UP, blocks are at rows 40-44, cols 39-43. Need to find the path to the target at the top.
- Plan: Move UP to reach the horizontal bar, then LEFT, then DOWN toward the white cross.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 35 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the full picture. The blocks are at rows 45-49, cols 34-38. Looking at the area:
```
40: .....#####..........###################
41: .....#####..........###################
42: .....#####..........###################
43: .....#####..........###################
44: .....#####..........###################
45: .....###############ooooo##############
46: .....###############ooooo##############
47: .....###############bbbbb##############
48: .....###############bbbbb##############
49: .....###############bbbbb##############
50: .......................................
```
The blocks are in a narrow gray corridor at rows 45-49, cols 27-52 (the ###############). The left gray area is at cols 19-23 (#####), and the passage is at cols 24-33 (..........).
So the blocks are in the bottom-right room, at the bottom of the maze. They can move UP through the right gray area, through the horizontal bar, and into the corridor or left gray area.
Now I need to figure out the efficient path to the white cross. The white cross is at rows 31-33, cols 21-22, in the left gray area.
From the current position (rows 45-49, cols 34-38), I need to:
1. Move UP to the horizontal bar (rows 25-29)
2. Move LEFT through the horizontal bar to the left side
3. Move DOWN into the left gray area to reach the white cross
But I've already wasted many actions. Let me think about the most efficient path.
From rows 45-49, I need to move UP to reach the horizontal bar. The right gray area is at rows 30-44, cols 34-52. The horizontal bar is at rows 25-29, cols 14-52. The corridor is at rows 17-24, cols 34-38.
To go from rows 45-49 to the horizontal bar, I need to move UP through the right gray area. The blocks are at rows 45-49, and the right gray area starts at row 30. So I need to move UP about 15 rows to reach the horizontal bar.
But earlier, when I moved UP 7 times from rows 40-44, the blocks ended up at rows 15-19 (in the corridor). So UP actions move the blocks quickly through the maze.
Let me try moving UP from the current position to see where the blocks end up. I'll move UP enough to reach the horizontal bar, then LEFT, then DOWN.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The blocks are at rows 45-49. To reach the horizontal bar (rows 25-29), I need to move UP about 16 rows. But earlier, 7 UP actions moved the blocks 25 rows (from 40-44 to 15-19). So UP actions seem to move the blocks more than 1 row per action.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine. Before UP 7, the blocks were at rows 40-44. After UP 7, they were at rows 15-19. That's a difference of 25 rows for 7 actions, which is about 3.6 rows per action. That seems odd.
Actually, I think I made an error earlier. Let me re-check the history. The blocks started at some position, and after various actions, they moved to different positions. But I don't have a clear record of the exact positions at each step.
Let me just try moving UP from the current position and see where the blocks end up. I'll move UP 5 times to start.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move UP to get to the horizontal bar, then LEFT
action(['UP'] * 5)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
